Petitioners Verified Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award
Off Calendar Mar-02-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. S. CLINTON WOODS OF THE BRADSHAW & ASSOCIATES, TEL. NO. 433-4800 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. RAY L. WONG & ATTY. KATHRYN KAWAICHI SCHULTZ OF THE DUANE MORRIS LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 957-3149 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PETITIONERS/ DEFENDANTS THE DOCTORS' COMPANY, THE DOCTORS' COMPANY INSURANCE SERVICES, LLC AND THE DOCTORS' MANAGEMENT COMPANY'S Verified Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award - The motion to confirm the arbitration award is granted and the motion to vacate is denied as moot. The "Final Award of Dismissal and Award of Costs" does not list Mr. Bradshaw and it is clear that he was not a party to the arbitration. The fact that the order granting the dispositive motion referred to Mr. Bradshaw does not change that finding. The complaint in this case indicates that Mt. Tam et. al.'s only claim related to the Cumis issue. Mt. Tam et. al. fail to clearly highlight what other issues the panel was supposed to arbitrate. There was no reservation of rights letter. Mr. Bradshaw even admitted that fact before the arbitrators. TDC stated that it would defend the action, but indemnity would not be available if plaintiff established some of its allegations. In other words, TDC was stating that indemnity/coverage would be denied if certain facts were established. There was no reservation of rights. Under CC 2860(b), there is no Cumis issue where coverage is denied. Mt. Tam et al. speculates that the Goldman communications would have made a difference on the Cumis issue. Mt. Tam et. al. does not meet the substantial prejudice standard of CCP 1286.2(a)(5). Mt. Tam et. al. does not demonstrate that the arbitration panels' use of the dispositive motion procedure was improper. The award should not be vacated on public policy grounds. There is no public policy violation where an arbitration panel finds, as a factual matter, that an insured is not entitled to Cumis counsel. This case does not present a public policy violation or illegality that warrants judicial scrutiny of an arbitration award. The court's prior ruling in the Goldman case, which is on appeal and not final, does not affect these proceedings. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sara Lerschen, CSR #6213 =302/ehg

Tam et. al. does not meet the substantial prejudice standard of CCP 1286.2(a)(5). Mt. Tam et. al. does not demonstrate that the arbitration panels' use of the dispositive motion procedure was improper. The award should not be vacated on public policy grounds. There is no public policy violation where an arbitration panel finds, as a factual matter, that an insured is not entitled to Cumis counsel. This case does not present a public policy violation or illegality that warrants judicial scrutiny of an arbitration award. The court's prior ruling in the Goldman case, which is on appeal and not final, does not affect these proceedings. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sara Lerschen, CSR #6213
